If possible, avoid creating environments that are very dark or very light.
Baking lighting into your model’s textures isn’t always possible, but when you use an unlit material instead of a PBR material, you improve your environment’s performance.
Try to use geometry that’s perpendicular to the viewer.
You can also use normal maps and use UV animation on your textures to create more convincing flat objects, like the pond shown in this video.
In the visonOS Simulator, once you have entered the immersive space, use the I/O > Set immersion to > 100% and then turn around to see the pond.
If your environment has no floor or ground, use a sphere instead of a dome.

Although the geometry detail in the stylized content of the sample isn’t dense enough to require much optimization, vertex count is one of the biggest sources of performance problems in rendered 3D scenes, so use the smallest number of vertices you need to achieve the effect that you want.
Avoid using them on large or distant objects and use them selectively when building immersive environments.

If somebody moves more than about a meter from their starting position, visionOS automatically turns immersive mode off, so you don’t need to worry about how environment objects look from other vantage points.
Try to avoid using long, thin triangles in your models.

The low-latency rendering pipeline consists of four main components: This architecture minimizes latency by using shared texture handles to avoid copying data between Metal and RealityKit.
The storage mode must be to create shared textures that reside entirely in GPU memory.

For stereo cameras, use that combines left and right eye textures: The method creates a plane with an unlit material displaying the camera texture.

In order to replicate that behavior, the shader has to set all of the properties used by to ensure that RealityKit renders the model correctly.
